g.data-narration: Welcome to Mass Storage Structures! Today we'll explore how operating systems manage physical storage devices like HDDs and SSDs efficiently.
g.text: Mass Storage Structures in Operating Systems

g.data-narration: Storage I/O is often the primary bottleneck in computer systems. Efficient storage management directly impacts system speed, reliability, and data integrity.
g.text: Storage I/O is often the PRIMARY

g.data-narration: Traditional Hard Disk Drives are mechanical devices with spinning platters coated with magnetic material. Read-write heads access data by moving across platter surfaces.
Disks are organized into tracks (concentric circles), sectors (track subdivisions, typically 512 bytes), and cylinders (same position across all platters).
Access time has three main components: seek time (head movement), rotational latency (waiting for sector), and transfer time (actual data read/write).
Solid-State Drives have no moving parts, use flash memory, and offer faster random access. However, they require wear leveling management for longevity.
g.text: Slower random access • No wear issues
No moving parts • Flash memory • Faster

g.data-narration: First-Come First-Served is the simplest scheduling algorithm. Requests are handled in order of arrival, but often results in inefficient random head movement.
g.text: Simple and fair, but can be inefficient. Requests: [95, 180, 35, 125, 15]

g.data-narration: Shortest Seek Time First selects the request closest to current head position, reducing movement. However, it can cause starvation for distant requests.
g.text: Head starts at 95. Always pick closest request to minimize seek time.
⚠️ Issue: Starvation possible for far requests

g.data-narration: The SCAN algorithm moves the head from one end to the other, servicing all requests in path. It then reverses direction, like an elevator. Prevents starvation.
g.text: Head moves end-to-end, servicing requests in path, then reverses. Like an elevator!

g.data-narration: C-SCAN is a variant where the head only services requests in one direction. Upon reaching the end, it returns to start without servicing. Provides uniform wait time.
g.text: Head services in ONE direction only, then jumps back to beginning. More uniform wait times.

g.data-narration: LOOK and C-LOOK are practical improvements. Instead of going to disk ends, LOOK reverses at the last request, and C-LOOK jumps to the first request.
g.text: Real implementations don't go to the very end - they reverse at the last request.
Reverses at LAST request in direction
Jumps to FIRST request, services one way
✓ More efficient than full SCAN - no unnecessary travel to disk edges

g.data-narration: OS handles disk formatting (sector preparation), partitioning (logical volumes), boot blocks (system startup), and bad block management (sector failures).
RAID uses multiple disks for reliability and performance. RAID 0 stripes for speed, RAID 1 mirrors for safety, RAID 5 uses distributed parity for both.
g.text: 💾 RAID - Redundant Array of Independent Disks
Combining multiple disks for performance and/or reliability through striping, mirroring, and parity.

g.data-narration: The primary goal is minimizing access time (especially seek time) and maximizing bandwidth. Scheduling algorithms trade off performance vs fairness.
g.text: • Goal: Minimize access time (seek time dominant) and maximize bandwidth
